title: Anesthesia for Cardiovascular Diseases MCQ – Chapter 18
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: What level of effort tolerance (in METS) is generally associated with lesser perioperative complications in patients with ischemic heart disease?
questions.explanation: As per the text, effort tolerance > 4 METS is generally associated with lesser complications in IHD patients undergoing surgery.
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Ischemic Heart Disease, Page 183

questions.question: How many parameters are included in the Lee revised cardiac risk index?
questions.explanation: The Lee revised cardiac risk index includes 6 parameters: high risk surgery, history of IHD, cerebrovascular disease, congestive heart failure, insulin dependent diabetes mellitus, and preoperative serum creatinine > 2 mg/dL.
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Ischemic Heart Disease, Page 183

questions.question: Regarding beta-blockers in IHD patients presenting for surgery, which statement is correct?

options.A: Beta-blockers should be stopped 5 days before surgery
options.B: Beta-blockers must not be stopped at any cost if the patient is already on them
options.C: Beta-blockers should be freshly started on the day before surgery
options.D: Beta-blockers should be replaced with calcium channel blockers preoperatively

questions.explanation: Beta-blockers must not be stopped at any cost if the patient is on them, as stopping can significantly increase morbidity and mortality. They should not be started afresh unless they can be started at least 1 week before surgery.
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Ischemic Heart Disease, Page 183

questions.question: How many days before surgery should clopidogrel be stopped in an IHD patient?
questions.explanation: Clopidogrel should be stopped 5 days before surgery. However, central neuraxial block can be given only after 7 days of stopping clopidogrel.
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Ischemic Heart Disease, Page 183

questions.question: For how long should elective surgery be deferred after placement of a conventional drug eluting stent?
questions.explanation: Elective surgery must be deferred for 6 months with new generation drug eluting stents and 1 year with conventional drug eluting stents.
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Ischemic Heart Disease, Page 183

questions.question: Which combination of ECG leads can detect 94–95% of intraoperative ischemic events?
questions.explanation: Lead V5 is best for detecting left ventricle infarction and lead II for arrhythmia (and inferior wall MI). A combination of lead II and V5 can detect 94–95% of intraoperative ischemic events.
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Ischemic Heart Disease, Page 183

questions.question: Which is the earliest and most sensitive monitor to detect intraoperative myocardial infarction?
questions.explanation: TEE is the earliest and most sensitive monitor to detect intraoperative infarction, capable of detecting more than 99% of ischemic events.
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Ischemic Heart Disease, Page 184

questions.question: A patient with IHD and normal LV function is undergoing surgery. Which of the following should be AVOIDED during maintenance of anesthesia?
questions.explanation: Nitrous oxide should be avoided in IHD patients for two reasons: it may cause mild sympathetic stimulation and it is a pulmonary vasoconstrictor. Inhalational agents can be safely used if LV function is normal.
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Ischemic Heart Disease, Page 184

questions.question: Mitral stenosis is considered severe when the transvalvular pressure gradient exceeds:
questions.explanation: Mitral stenosis is considered severe if the transvalvular pressure gradient is > 10 mm Hg (normal < 5 mm Hg).

questions.question: Why should tachycardia be avoided in patients with mitral stenosis?

options.A: It increases the afterload on the left ventricle
options.C: It decreases diastole, reducing left ventricular filling and hence cardiac output
options.D: It increases the degree of mitral regurgitation

questions.explanation: In mitral stenosis, tachycardia decreases diastole which reduces left ventricular filling and hence stroke volume and cardiac output.

questions.question: Approximately what fraction of mitral stenosis patients have atrial fibrillation?
questions.explanation: Atrial fibrillation is seen in one-third of the patients with mitral stenosis, and a significant number will be on oral anticoagulants contraindicating the use of CNB.

questions.question: Mitral regurgitation is considered severe when the regurgitant fraction exceeds:
questions.explanation: Mitral regurgitation is said to be severe if the regurgitant fraction is > 0.6.

questions.question: In mitral regurgitation, which hemodynamic change should be avoided?
questions.explanation: In MR, bradycardia should be avoided because it increases diastole which increases the regurgitation. Hypertension should also be avoided as it increases afterload.

questions.question: Which antihypertensives should be stopped on the day of surgery in hypertensive patients?

options.B: ACE inhibitors and angiotensin II receptor antagonists

questions.explanation: All antihypertensives should be continued except ACE inhibitors and angiotensin II receptor antagonists, which are stopped on the day of surgery as they can cause profound intraoperative hypotension.

questions.question: In mitral valve prolapse (MVP), which assessment is MOST important preoperatively?

options.B: Whether MVP is associated with mitral regurgitation or not

questions.explanation: The hemodynamic management principles of MVP without MR and with MR are different, so it is very important to assess preoperatively whether MVP is associated with MR or not.

questions.question: Which induction agent is the agent of choice for shock patients?
questions.explanation: Ketamine is the induction agent of choice for shock patients due to its sympathomimetic effects. However, in chronic/severe shock with sympathetic depletion, its direct myocardial depressant effect may predominate.

questions.question: Among valvular lesions, which has the most devastating complications including sudden death?
questions.explanation: Among the valvular lesions, aortic stenosis has the most devastating complications like infarction, arrhythmia or even sudden death.

questions.question: Aortic stenosis is considered severe when the aortic valve orifice is less than:
questions.explanation: Severe AS is defined as transvalvular gradient > 50 mm Hg or aortic valve orifice less than 0.8 cm².

questions.question: Which vasopressor is the agent of choice for patients with aortic stenosis, and why?

options.A: Ephedrine, because it increases cardiac output
options.B: Noradrenaline, because it has strong alpha effect
options.C: Phenylephrine, because it does not cause tachycardia
options.D: Dopamine, because it improves renal blood flow

questions.explanation: As phenylephrine does not cause tachycardia, it is the vasopressor of choice for patients with aortic stenosis.

questions.question: In aortic regurgitation, which hemodynamic changes should be avoided?
questions.explanation: In AR, bradycardia by increasing diastole and hypertension by increasing afterload can increase the regurgitant fraction, so both should be avoided.

questions.question: Which type of prosthetic heart valve is more prone to thromboembolism but has a longer life?
questions.explanation: Mechanical prosthetic heart valves are more prone for thromboembolism but have a long life of 20–30 years, while bioprosthetic valves are less prone for thromboembolism but have shorter life of 10–15 years.
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Prosthetic Heart Valves, Page 186

questions.question: How many days before surgery should warfarin be stopped in patients with prosthetic heart valves?
questions.explanation: Warfarin should be stopped 5 days before surgery and heparin should be started, which is then stopped 12–24 hours before surgery.
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Prosthetic Heart Valves, Page 186

questions.question: According to new guidelines, antibiotic prophylaxis for patients with prosthetic heart valves is recommended for which procedures?

options.C: Dental procedures involving gingiva/oral mucosa/periapical region and invasive procedures involving incision on infective tissue

questions.explanation: The new guidelines recommend antibiotic prophylaxis only for dental procedures involving gingiva, oral mucosa and periapical region of teeth, and invasive procedures involving incision on infective tissue.
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Prosthetic Heart Valves, Page 186

questions.question: What is the basic aim of anesthetic management in left to right shunts (VSD, ASD, PDA)?

options.A: Maintain high SVR and low PVR
options.B: Maintain low SVR and high PVR
options.C: Maintain high SVR and high PVR
options.D: Maintain low SVR and low PVR

questions.explanation: In left to right shunts, increase in SVR or decrease in PVR increases the shunt fraction. The aim is to maintain low systemic vascular resistance and high pulmonary vascular resistance.
questions.topic: Congenital Heart Diseases – Left to Right Shunts
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Congenital Heart Diseases, Page 187

questions.question: For which congenital defect is antibiotic prophylaxis for infective endocarditis recommended ONLY if there is a concomitant valvular abnormality?
questions.explanation: Antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended for VSD and PDA, but for ASD only if there is a concomitant valvular abnormality (mitral valve prolapse may be associated with ASD).
questions.topic: Congenital Heart Diseases – Left to Right Shunts
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Congenital Heart Diseases, Page 187

questions.question: In left to right shunts, what effect does increased pulmonary blood flow have on IV induction agents?

options.B: Causes dilution but has no clinical implication
options.D: Causes unpredictable effects requiring dose reduction

questions.explanation: Although increase in pulmonary blood flow causes dilution of IV agents in left to right shunts, this has no clinical implication.
questions.topic: Congenital Heart Diseases – Left to Right Shunts
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Congenital Heart Diseases, Page 187

questions.question: A patient in shock must be resuscitated to at least what minimum blood pressure before considering anesthesia?

options.A: MAP > 50 mm Hg or SBP > 80 mm Hg
options.B: MAP > 65 mm Hg or SBP > 90 mm Hg
options.C: MAP > 75 mm Hg or SBP > 100 mm Hg
options.D: MAP > 80 mm Hg or SBP > 110 mm Hg

questions.explanation: Patient must be resuscitated to at least mean arterial pressure > 65 mm Hg or systolic pressure > 90 mm Hg before considering anesthesia.

questions.question: Which is the induction agent of choice for right to left shunts (cyanotic heart diseases)?
questions.explanation: As ketamine increases the systemic vascular resistance, it is the agent of choice for induction in right to left shunts.
questions.topic: Congenital Heart Diseases – Right to Left Shunts
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Congenital Heart Diseases, Page 187

questions.question: What happens to the onset of IV induction agents in right to left shunts?

options.B: Onset is rapid as pulmonary circulation is bypassed
options.D: Onset is slower due to polycythemia

questions.explanation: In right to left shunts, as pulmonary circulation is bypassed, the induction with IV agents will be rapid.
questions.topic: Congenital Heart Diseases – Right to Left Shunts
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Congenital Heart Diseases, Page 187

questions.question: Which muscle relaxant is the agent of choice in right to left shunts, and why?

options.A: Vecuronium, because it is cardiac stable
options.B: Atracurium, because it does not affect SVR
options.D: Succinylcholine, because of its rapid onset

questions.explanation: Pancuronium causes hypertension (increases SVR), which is desirable in right to left shunts to reduce shunt fraction, making it the muscle relaxant of choice.
questions.topic: Congenital Heart Diseases – Right to Left Shunts
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Congenital Heart Diseases, Page 187

questions.question: In right to left shunts, the FiO₂ used is 50% instead of 33% because:

options.B: High FiO₂ decreases PVR, which is beneficial
options.D: N₂O at higher concentrations is toxic

questions.explanation: High FiO₂ decreases PVR, which is beneficial in right to left shunts (aim is to decrease PVR), so oxygen concentration used is 50%.
questions.topic: Congenital Heart Diseases – Right to Left Shunts
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Congenital Heart Diseases, Page 187

questions.question: In coarctation of aorta, what is the minimum mean arterial pressure that should be maintained in the lower limb?
questions.explanation: In coarctation of aorta, blood pressure monitoring in lower limbs is mandatory and mean arterial pressure of the lower limb should remain at least > 40 mm Hg.
questions.topic: Congenital Heart Diseases – Coarctation of Aorta
questions.reference: Ch 18, Section: Congenital Heart Diseases, Page 187

questions.question: Regarding heart failure patients, which statement is correct?

options.A: Elective surgery can proceed if LVEF > 30%
options.B: Elective surgery is contraindicated in heart failure patients
options.D: Beta-blockers must be stopped before surgery

questions.explanation: The patient in heart failure carries very high risk of perioperative morbidity and mortality, therefore elective surgery is contraindicated.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of right heart failure?
questions.explanation: The most common cause of right heart failure is left heart failure.

questions.question: Which drugs remain the primary treatment for chronic heart failure?

options.C: ACE inhibitors, ARBs and aldosterone antagonists (RAAS inhibitors)

questions.explanation: The RAAS system plays an important role in heart failure pathophysiology. ACE inhibitors, ARBs and aldosterone antagonists remain the primary drugs for treatment of chronic heart failure.

questions.question: Which preoperative electrolyte is particularly important to check in heart failure patients on diuretics and digitalis?
questions.explanation: As heart failure patients are on diuretics and digitalis, checking preoperative electrolytes, especially potassium, is a must.

questions.question: Why is positive pressure ventilation beneficial in heart failure patients?
questions.explanation: Positive pressure ventilation is beneficial in heart failure by decreasing the venous return (preload), which is one of the basic principles of heart failure management.

questions.question: What is the most common genetic cardiovascular disease?
questions.explanation: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), also called subaortic stenosis, is the most common genetic cardiovascular disease.

questions.question: What is the most important feature of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM)?

options.B: Dynamic left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) obstruction
options.D: Systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve

questions.explanation: Dynamic left ventricular outflow obstruction (LVOT) is the most important feature of HCM.

questions.question: Pulmonary edema in HCM patients CANNOT be treated by diuretics, nitrates, and digitalis. What is the recommended treatment?
questions.explanation: In HCM, pulmonary edema cannot be treated by diuretics (decrease preload), nitrates (decrease afterload), or digitalis (increase contractility) as all worsen LVOT obstruction. It is treated by vasopressors (phenylephrine) and β-blockers (esmolol).

questions.question: Why is pancuronium contraindicated in HCM?

options.B: It can cause sympathetic stimulation which worsens LVOT obstruction
options.D: It has a prolonged duration of action

questions.explanation: Pancuronium is contraindicated in HCM because it can cause sympathetic stimulation, which increases myocardial contractility and worsens LVOT obstruction.

questions.question: Which is the most common type of cardiomyopathy?
questions.explanation: Dilated cardiomyopathy is the most common cardiomyopathy. It may be primary (idiopathic) or secondary like alcoholic or peripartum cardiomyopathy.

questions.question: In patients with pericardial diseases (constrictive pericarditis/cardiac tamponade), which induction agent is preferred?
questions.explanation: In pericardial diseases, ketamine is preferred for induction as it stimulates the sympathetic system and increases cardiac output in these low cardiac output states.

questions.question: In pericardial diseases, which of the following statements is correct regarding anesthetic technique?

options.A: Central neuraxial blocks are the technique of choice
options.B: Pericardiocentesis should be done under GA before the procedure
options.C: CNB (spinal/epidural) are contraindicated and pericardiocentesis should be done under local anesthesia before GA
options.D: Only monitored anesthesia care is appropriate

questions.explanation: Patients with pericardial diseases are in low cardiac output state, so CNB are contraindicated. Pericardiocentesis should be done under local anesthesia before proceeding to GA.

questions.question: Which muscle relaxant is preferred for patients with pericardial diseases, and why?

options.A: Vecuronium, because it is cardiac stable
options.B: Pancuronium, because it stimulates sympathetic system and increases cardiac output
options.C: Atracurium, because it causes histamine release

questions.explanation: Pancuronium is preferred for pericardial diseases because it stimulates the sympathetic system and increases cardiac output, which is needed in the low cardiac output state.

questions.question: What is the most common indication for cardiac implanted electronic devices (CIED)?
questions.explanation: CIED are used for treating bradyarrhythmias, with sick sinus syndrome being the most common indication.

questions.question: What is the most common source of electromagnetic interference (EMI) that can cause CIED malfunction during surgery?
questions.explanation: The most common source of EMI is cautery, followed by radiofrequency devices and MRI. MRI is contraindicated for patients with CIED.

questions.question: Before surgery, CIED should be programmed to asynchronous mode. In this mode, the pacemaker delivers a fixed rate of approximately:
questions.explanation: In asynchronous mode, pacemakers deliver a fixed rate of 70–72 beats/minute irrespective of the patient's own rate, avoiding the risk of CIED being affected by interference.

questions.question: Hypertension is defined as blood pressure exceeding which value on at least 2 occasions?
questions.explanation: Hypertension is defined as blood pressure > 140/90 on at least 2 occasions 1–2 weeks apart, while hypertensive crisis is defined as BP > 180/120.

questions.question: What is the general consensus for the diastolic BP threshold to delay elective surgery in hypertensive patients?
questions.explanation: Although there is no universal standard cut-off, the general consensus is to delay elective surgery if diastolic blood pressure is > 110 mm Hg.
